lines.translation: Les vacances d'hiver approchent à grands pas. Ces derniers jours, Zhang Tianming a réfléchi à l'idée de faire un voyage. Ses parents veulent qu'il visite leur ville natale, Nankin. Zhang Tianming aimerait aussi être le guide touristique de Lisa et l'emmener dans quelques endroits en Chine. En dehors de Nankin, où devraient-ils aller d'autre ? Pour décider de l'itinéraire, Zhang Tianming a sorti une carte de la Chine et a commencé à l'examiner.
Lisa, viens jeter un coup d'œil à la carte de la Chine. Voyons où ce serait bien de voyager.
Regarder une carte ? C'est une bonne idée. J'avais aussi envie d'apprendre un peu la géographie chinoise. Je connais trop peu la géographie de la Chine.
Allons d'abord à Nankin, la ville natale de mes parents... C'est ici, dans le sud-est de la Chine. Ensuite, où veux-tu aller ?
C'est Harbin, n'est-ce pas ? Allons à Harbin.
Tu veux passer du sud-est à l'extrême nord d'un coup ?
Les lanternes de glace de Harbin ont été montrées à la télé hier. Elles sont magnifiques.
Les lanternes de glace sont belles, c'est vrai, mais il fait incroyablement froid à Harbin en ce moment. Allons-y en été.
D'accord. Si on ne va pas au nord, alors prenons un train vers l'ouest. J'ai entendu dire que le Xinjiang est un endroit très spécial.

Le Xinjiang est au nord-ouest. Il commence à faire froid en octobre, donc ce n'est pas le bon moment pour y aller maintenant. De plus, prendre le train prend trop de temps. Allons-y la prochaine fois !
Voici le fleuve Yangtsé, et voici le fleuve Jaune. Prenons un bateau pour admirer les paysages du fleuve Yangtsé ou du fleuve Jaune.
Un voyage en bateau prendrait beaucoup de temps. La plupart des fleuves chinois coulent d'ouest en est. Si nous voyageons d'est en ouest, cela prendra encore plus de temps.
Pourquoi la plupart des fleuves chinois coulent-ils d'ouest en est ?
La partie ouest de la Chine est constituée de plateaux ; on y trouve la plus haute montagne du monde. En revanche, à l'est, il y a des plaines et l'océan.

Il semble que les grandes villes soient toutes proches de la côte ou non loin de la mer. Regarde, au nord, il y a Pékin et Tianjin ; au sud-est, voici Shanghai et Nankin ; et ici au sud, il y a Canton et Shenzhen.

Tu as tout à fait raison. La population chinoise se trouve principalement à l'est et au sud. Quant au sud-ouest, il y a de nombreuses grandes montagnes et des plateaux. Le nord-ouest, en revanche, compte de nombreux déserts avec des conditions naturelles défavorables. C'est pourquoi la population y est relativement clairsemée.
Je pense que la topographie de la Chine ressemble un peu à celle de l'Amérique.

Tu as raison. De plus, la Chine et les États-Unis ont une superficie similaire, mais la population chinoise est plus de quatre fois supérieure à celle des États-Unis. J'ai entendu dire que pendant les jours fériés ou les vacances, les sites touristiques en Chine sont bondés de monde, il y a une foule immense.
Vraiment ?
Heureusement, quand nous arriverons en Chine, les étudiants ne seront pas encore en vacances, et ce ne sera ni le Nouvel An ni d'autres jours fériés.
La Chine est si grande, et nos vacances d'hiver sont si courtes. Quel serait le meilleur endroit à visiter pour nous ?
Je pense qu'il n'y a pas de meilleur endroit où aller que le Yunnan.
Où est le Yunnan ? ... Je l'ai trouvé. Le voici. C'est une province du sud-ouest. Il ne fait pas froid là-bas en hiver ?
J'ai entendu dire que certains endroits au Yunnan sont comme le printemps toute l'année, ni trop froids ni trop chauds.
Je me souviens avoir entendu à la télé que le Yunnan a de beaux paysages, de nombreuses minorités ethniques, et que c'est un endroit formidable pour le tourisme.
D'accord, le Yunnan. C'est décidé. Nous irons au Yunnan.

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:旅行] et [zh:旅游] sont à peu près synonymes. Le terme [zh:旅行] n'implique pas en soi un but de voyage, et les agences de voyage sont toujours des [zh:旅行社]. Cependant, [zh:游] signifie errer, donc [zh:旅游] implique spécifiquement de voyager pour le plaisir. Voyager pour affaires se dit [zh:出差] [zh:chū chāi]. Les touristes sont des [zh:游客]. À Taïwan, le terme [zh:观光客] [zh:guānguāngkè] est également utilisé ; [zh:观光] signifie « faire du tourisme ».

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:河流] est un nom collectif. Il peut désigner les rivières en général ou toutes les rivières d'une zone géographique. [zh:河] peut désigner une rivière spécifique ou plusieurs rivières.

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:部] est utilisé avec d'autres caractères pour former des mots polysyllabiques comme [zh:东部], [zh:南部], [zh:西南部]. Il n'est pas utilisé seul. Le terme [zh:中国的东部], qui signifie la partie orientale de la Chine, fait référence à la région est à l'intérieur de la Chine. En revanche, [zh:中国的东边] peut désigner soit « l'Est de la Chine », c'est-à-dire à l'intérieur de la Chine, soit « à l'est de la Chine », c'est-à-dire en dehors de la Chine.

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:少数民族] signifie « minorité ethnique », littéralement « nationalité minoritaire ».

grammar_sections.title: Indiquer le début d'un état avec 起来
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Comme nous l'avons appris dans IC2, 起来 peut indiquer qu'une action commence. Il peut aussi être utilisé après des adjectifs pour marquer le début et la continuation d'un état. Quand un verbe est suivi d'un objet, celui-ci doit être placé entre 起 et 来. On utilise souvent 起来 avec des adjectifs comme 亮 [zh:liàng] qui signifie lumineux, 大 [zh:dà] qui signifie grand, 高 [zh:gāo] qui signifie haut, 多 [zh:duō] qui signifie nombreux, 快 [zh:kuài] qui signifie rapide, et 热闹 [zh:rènao] qui signifie animé. Comme 起来, le complément 下来 peut aussi suivre un verbe ou un adjectif pour indiquer le début d'une action ou d'une condition, mais il suggère généralement une diminution ou une baisse de taille, de volume, de quantité, de vitesse, d'intensité, et ainsi de suite. Par exemple, on utilise souvent 下来 avec 暗 [zh:àn] qui signifie sombre, 小 [zh:xiǎo] qui signifie petit, 低 [zh:dī] qui signifie bas, 少 [zh:shǎo] qui signifie peu nombreux, 慢 [zh:màn] qui signifie lent, 安静 [zh:ānjìng] qui signifie calme, et 停 [zh:tíng] qui signifie arrêter. Les changements météorologiques saisonniers peuvent aussi être exprimés avec 起来.

usage_notes: 起来 peut suivre un verbe pour indiquer qu'une action commence.
Quand un verbe qui prend un objet est utilisé avec 起来, l'objet doit être placé entre 起 et 来.
起来 peut aussi être utilisé après un adjectif pour indiquer le début et la continuation d'un état.

On utilise souvent 起来 avec des adjectifs qui suggèrent une augmentation ou une amélioration, comme 亮 [zh:liàng] qui signifie lumineux, 大 [zh:dà] qui signifie grand, 高 [zh:gāo] qui signifie haut, 多 [zh:duō] qui signifie nombreux, 快 [zh:kuài] qui signifie rapide, et 热闹 [zh:rènao] qui signifie animé.

En revanche, 下来 indique le début d'une condition qui suggère une diminution ou une baisse. On l'utilise souvent avec des adjectifs comme 慢 [zh:màn] qui signifie lent, 暗 [zh:àn] qui signifie sombre, 小 [zh:xiǎo] qui signifie petit, et 安静 [zh:ānjìng] qui signifie calme.

Les changements météorologiques saisonniers peuvent être exprimés avec 起来, par exemple 夏天热起来了 [zh:xiàtiān rè qǐlái le] qui signifie l'été commence à devenir chaud, et 冬天冷起来了 [zh:dōngtiān lěng qǐlái le] qui signifie l'hiver commence à devenir froid.

grammar_sections.title: La conjonction 而
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 而 est utilisé pour relier deux propositions qui présentent des caractéristiques ou des situations différentes ou contrastées. Il a une connotation littéraire.

patterns.meaning_en: Relie deux propositions avec des caractéristiques ou des situations différentes ou contrastées.

examples.hanzi: 中国西部是高原,那儿有世界上最高的山。而东边是平原和大海。
examples.pinyin: Zhōngguó xībù shì gāoyuán, nàr yǒu shìjiè shàng zuìgāo de shān. Ér dōngbiān shì píngyuán hé dàhǎi.
examples.translation: L'ouest de la Chine est constitué de plateaux, où l'on trouve la plus haute montagne du monde. En revanche, à l'est, il y a des plaines et l'océan.

examples.hanzi: 小林毕业以后计划回中国工作,而不准备留在美国。
examples.pinyin: Xiǎo Lín bìyè yǐhòu jìhuà huí Zhōngguó gōngzuò, ér bù zhǔnbèi liú zài Měiguó.
examples.translation: La petite Lin prévoit de retourner travailler en Chine plutôt que de rester aux États-Unis.

examples.hanzi: 我想去学校图书馆打工,而我的同屋想去校外打工。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng qù xuéxiào túshūguǎn dǎgōng, ér wǒ de tóngwū xiǎng qù xiàowài dǎgōng.
examples.translation: Je veux travailler à la bibliothèque de l'école, tandis que mon colocataire veut travailler en dehors du campus.

examples.hanzi: 我哥哥已经拿到硕士学位了,而我才高中毕业。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ gēge yǐjīng nádào shuòshì xuéwèi le, ér wǒ cái gāozhōng bìyè.
examples.translation: Mon frère aîné a déjà obtenu sa maîtrise. Moi, en revanche, je viens tout juste de terminer le lycée.

grammar_sections.title: le plus [adjectif] qui soit
grammar_sections.explanation_en: L'expression « X最adjective 不过了 » a le même sens que « 没有比X更adjective 的 », signifiant que rien ne peut surpasser X. C'est une tournure de phrase assez forte.

patterns.meaning_en: rien n'est plus... que... ; ...est le meilleur

examples.hanzi: 她过生日,买花送她最好不过了。
examples.pinyin: Tā guò shēngrì, mǎi huā sòng tā zuì hǎo búguò le.
examples.translation: Rien ne serait un plus beau cadeau pour son anniversaire que des fleurs.

examples.hanzi: 这本书对世界经济的介绍最清楚不过了。
examples.pinyin: Zhè běn shū duì shìjiè jīngjì de jièshào zuì qīngchu búguò le.
examples.translation: L'introduction de ce livre sur l'économie mondiale est d'une clarté inégalée.

examples.hanzi: 这个实习工作对化学系的学生来说,最合适不过了。
examples.pinyin: Zhège shíxí gōngzuò duì huàxué xì de xuéshēng lái shuō, zuì héshì búguò le.
examples.translation: Pour un étudiant en chimie, ce stage est le plus approprié qui soit.

grammar_sections.title: La particule dynamique [zh:过] revisitée
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Comme nous l'avons appris dans IC2, la particule dynamique [zh:过] est employée pour exprimer qu'une personne a déjà fait quelque chose. Elle se distingue de [zh:了] de deux façons. Premièrement, [zh:了] est de nature descriptive et sert à rapporter la survenue d'une action. [zh:过], quant à elle, est de nature explicative. Elle est utilisée pour justifier une affirmation qui suit. Autrement dit, l'accent est mis sur l'impact qu'une action passée a eu sur la situation actuelle. Toutes les propositions contenant [zh:过] fournissent un contexte explicatif aux propositions qui les suivent. [zh:过] indique l'impact ou l'influence d'une action passée sur le présent. Deuxièmement, [zh:了] requiert généralement une expression de temps spécifique, sauf si la phrase s'inscrit dans un récit plus large où le contexte temporel a déjà été mentionné ou est implicitement compris ; [zh:过] n'en a pas besoin. Lorsque nous utilisons [zh:过], le moment impliqué est souvent assez vague : « avant » ou « dans le passé ». Ce n'est que lorsque nous souhaitons être plus précis, généralement pour réfuter ou appuyer un argument, que nous employons une expression de temps.

examples.hanzi: 我去年去了一次中国，在北京实习了三个月，别的地方都没去，就回来了。
examples.translation: L'année dernière, je suis allé en Chine pour un stage de trois mois à Pékin. Je n'ai vraiment visité aucun autre endroit, puis je suis rentré.

examples.hanzi: 第二天早上，我很早就起来了，起床后就去运动场跑步。
examples.translation: Le lendemain matin, je me suis levé très tôt. Une fois levé, je suis allé faire mon jogging au stade.

examples.hanzi: 客人们进来以后，找到了自己的位子，坐了下来。
examples.translation: Les invités sont entrés, ont trouvé leurs sièges et se sont installés.

examples.hanzi: (以前)我们在一起学过英文，我知道他英文很好。
examples.translation: Nous avons étudié l'anglais ensemble. Je sais qu'il a un très bon niveau d'anglais, justement parce que nous avons étudié ensemble.

examples.hanzi:
你去过中国，请告诉我们去哪儿旅游最好。
云南的旅游景点多，少数民族的文化也很有意思，是旅游的好地方。
examples.translation: Tu es allé en Chine. Donne-nous des conseils sur les meilleurs endroits à visiter. Puisque tu y es allé, tu peux nous indiquer les meilleurs endroits. Le Yunnan regorge de sites touristiques, et les cultures des groupes ethniques minoritaires y sont également fascinantes. C'est un excellent endroit à visiter.

examples.hanzi: 他学过好几年中文，都能看中文报纸、杂志了。
examples.translation: Il étudie le chinois depuis plusieurs années. Il peut même lire des journaux et des magazines chinois. C'est parce qu'il étudie le chinois depuis plusieurs années qu'il peut comprendre les journaux et magazines chinois.

examples.hanzi:
我听说你没去过别的国家，是真的吗？
谁说的？我去年去过日本。
examples.translation: J'ai entendu dire que tu n'es jamais allé à l'étranger. C'est vrai ? Qui a dit ça ? Je suis allé au Japon l'année dernière.

usage_notes: Lorsque [zh:过] est utilisé dans le sens expérientiel, il ne peut généralement pas être suivi de [zh:了].

grammar_sections.title: [zh:为了] et [zh:因为]
grammar_sections.explanation_en: [zh:为了] indique le but, tandis que [zh:因为] indique la cause.

examples.hanzi: 为了把中文学得更好,我明年要去中国留学。
examples.translation: Afin d'améliorer mon chinois, je vais étudier en Chine l'année prochaine.

examples.hanzi: 因为在中国学中文条件更好,所以我明年去中国留学。
examples.translation: Parce que les conditions en Chine sont meilleures pour apprendre le chinois, je vais y étudier l'année prochaine.

examples.hanzi: 为了解决学费的问题,他一边上学一边打工。
examples.translation: Afin de résoudre le problème des frais de scolarité, il travaille à temps partiel tout en allant à l'école.

examples.hanzi: 因为得交学费,所以他一边上学一边打工。
examples.translation: Parce qu'il doit payer ses frais de scolarité, il travaille à temps partiel tout en allant à l'école.

examples.hanzi: 因为这个学校很有名,所以我申请。
examples.translation: Parce que cette école est très célèbre, j'y postule.

examples.hanzi: 我申请这个学校,是为了能跟我的女朋友在一起。
examples.translation: Je postule à cette école pour pouvoir être avec ma petite amie.

grammar_sections.title: Comparaison entre [zh:理解] et [zh:了解]
grammar_sections.explanation_en: [zh:理解] signifie « comprendre ». [zh:了解], en revanche, signifie avoir une connaissance claire de quelque chose.

examples.hanzi: 她现在学习压力大,不能帮助我,我理解。
examples.pinyin: Tā xiànzài xuéxí yālì dà, bù néng bāngzhù wǒ, wǒ lǐjiě.
examples.translation: Elle a beaucoup de pression avec ses études et ne peut pas m'aider. Je comprends.

examples.hanzi: 老师刚才说的意思,你没有完全理解。
examples.pinyin: Lǎoshī gāngcái shuō de yìsi, nǐ méiyǒu wánquán lǐjiě.
examples.translation: Tu n'as pas entièrement compris ce que le professeur vient de dire.

examples.hanzi: 做这件事很容易,我不理解你为什么怕这怕那。
examples.pinyin: Zuò zhè jiàn shì hěn róngyì, wǒ bù lǐjiě nǐ wèishéme pà zhè pà nà.
examples.translation: C'est très facile à faire. Je ne comprends pas pourquoi tu es si timide.

examples.hanzi: 我在那个国家住过几年,对那里比较了解。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ zài nàge guójiā zhùguo jǐ nián, duì nàli bǐjiào liǎojiě.
examples.translation: J'ai vécu dans ce pays pendant quelques années et j'en connais bien les rouages.

examples.hanzi: 你了解她的过去吗？
examples.pinyin: Nǐ liǎojiě tā de guòqù ma?
examples.translation: Connaissez-vous son passé ?

examples.hanzi: 我们老师对每个学生在学习上的问题都很了解。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen lǎoshī duì měi ge xuéshēng zài xuéxí shàng de wèntí dōu hěn liǎojiě.
examples.translation: Notre professeur connaît bien les difficultés d'apprentissage de chaque élève.

grammar_sections.title: [zh:一下子] (tout d'un coup, en un instant)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: [zh:一下子] est équivalent à [zh:一下]. Cela signifie « peu de temps s'est écoulé » et est souvent utilisé pour décrire la rapidité avec laquelle des actions ou des événements se produisent.

examples.hanzi: 你一下子就要从南边跑到最北边去？
examples.translation: Tout d'un coup, vous voulez aller du sud à l'extrême nord ?

examples.hanzi: 你说的那个商店我一下子就找到了，不难找。
examples.translation: J'ai trouvé le magasin dont vous parliez tout de suite. Ce n'était pas difficile à trouver.

examples.hanzi: 妈妈给他的零用钱他一下子就花完了。
examples.translation: Il a dépensé l'argent de poche que sa mère lui avait donné en un rien de temps.

grammar_sections.title: [zh:大多] (principalement)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: [zh:大多] est un adverbe qui signifie « pour la plupart » ou « principalement ».

examples.hanzi: 我们班的同学大多住在学校宿舍。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen bān de tóngxué dàduō zhù zài xuéxiào sùshè.
examples.translation: Nos camarades de classe vivent principalement dans les dortoirs de l'école.

examples.hanzi: 美国的大山和河流的名字，我弟弟大多说不对。
examples.pinyin: Měiguó de dàshān hé héliú de míngzì, wǒ dìdi dàduō shuō bu duì.
examples.translation: Mon jeune frère n'arrivait pas à se souvenir correctement des noms de la plupart des grandes montagnes et rivières des États-Unis.

examples.hanzi: 我们学校教授的研究，我大多都了解，你不用上网看了。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen xuéxiào jiàoshòu de yánjiū, wǒ dàduō dōu liǎojiě, nǐ búyòng shàngwǎng kàn le.
examples.translation: Je connais bien les recherches de la plupart des professeurs de notre école. Vous n'avez pas besoin d'aller en ligne pour vous renseigner.

usage_notes: Il ne peut pas être utilisé avant les noms.

grammar_sections.title: Indiquer une pause dans le discours avec [zh:呢]
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Cette particule se place après un sujet ou un thème et est suivie d'une pause dans le discours. Elle apparaît généralement dans des phrases énumératives qui suggèrent un contraste.

patterns.meaning_en: Utilisé pour marquer une pause après un thème, souvent dans un contexte énumératif ou contrastif.

examples.hanzi: 你说的没错,中国的人口主要在东部和南部。西南呢,高山、高原多,西北呢,沙漠多,自然条件不太好,所以人口比较少。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ shuō de méi cuò, Zhōngguó de rénkǒu zhǔyào zài dōngbù hé nánbù. Xīnán ne, gāoshān, gāoyuán duō, xīběi ne, shāmò duō, zìrán tiáojiàn bú tài hǎo, suǒyǐ rénkǒu bǐjiào shǎo.
examples.translation: Vous l'avez bien dit. La population chinoise se trouve principalement à l'est et au sud. Quant au sud-ouest, il y a de nombreuses grandes montagnes et des plateaux. Le nord-ouest, en revanche, a beaucoup de déserts avec des conditions naturelles défavorables. C'est pourquoi la population y est relativement clairsemée.

examples.hanzi:
Q: 你们寒假准备做什么?
A: 我呢,要回家,小张呢,要留在学校打工,小李要做什么,我不知道。
examples.pinyin:
Q: Nǐmen hánjià zhǔnbèi zuò shénme?
A: Wǒ ne, yào huí jiā, Xiǎo Zhāng ne, yào liú zài xuéxiào dǎgōng, Xiǎo Lǐ yào zuò shénme, wǒ bù zhīdào.
examples.translation:
Q : Que comptez-vous faire pendant les vacances d'hiver ?
R : Je rentre chez moi. Petit Zhang, lui, doit rester à l'école et travailler. Je ne sais pas ce que fera Petit Li.

examples.hanzi: 选什么专业好,我还没想清楚。学医呢,没有意思,学文学呢,怕以后不好找工作。
examples.pinyin: Xuǎn shénme zhuānyè hǎo, wǒ hái méi xiǎng qīngchu. Xué yī ne, méiyǒu yìsi, xué wénxué ne, pà yǐhòu bù hǎo zhǎo gōngzuò.
examples.translation: Quelle filière choisir ? Je n'ai pas encore trouvé. D'un côté, étudier la médecine est ennuyeux. D'un autre côté, j'ai peur de ne pas trouver de travail si j'étudie la littérature.

usage_notes: Cette particule se place après un sujet ou un thème et est suivie d'une pause dans le discours. Elle apparaît généralement dans des phrases énumératives qui suggèrent un contraste.

context_examples.cn: 我也正想学学中国地理呢。
context_examples.py: Wǒ yě zhèng xiǎng xuéxue Zhōngguó dìlǐ ne.
context_examples.translation: J'ai aussi envie d'en apprendre un peu sur la géographie chinoise.

context_examples.cn: 他的父母希望他去他们的家乡南京看看。
context_examples.py: Tā de fùmǔ xīwàng tā qù tāmen de jiāxiāng Nánjīng kànkan.
context_examples.translation: Ses parents veulent qu'il visite leur ville natale, Nankin.

context_examples.cn: 为了决定旅行的路线,张天明拿出一张中国地图,看了起来。
context_examples.py: Wèile juédìng lǚxíng de lùxiàn, Zhāng Tiānmíng ná chū yì zhāng Zhōngguó dìtú, kàn le qǐlái.
context_examples.translation: Pour décider de l'itinéraire, Zhang Tianming a sorti une carte de la Chine et a commencé à la regarder.

context_examples.py: Wǒ duì Zhōngguó dìlǐ liǎojiě de tài shǎo le.

context_examples.translation: Prenons un bateau pour admirer les paysages du fleuve Yangtsé ou du fleuve Jaune.

context_examples.cn: 中国的河流,大多从西往东流,如果从东往西走,时间更长。

context_examples.translation: La plupart des fleuves chinois coulent d'ouest en est. Si nous voyageons d'est en ouest, cela prendra encore plus de temps.

context_examples.translation: La plupart des fleuves chinois coulent d'ouest en est. Si nous voyageons d'est en ouest, cela prendra encore plus de temps.

global_config.visual_style: Cinematic, realistic style with warm, natural lighting, reminiscent of a modern slice-of-life film.
global_config.setting_summary_cn: 一间舒适的大学宿舍。张天明和丽莎正在计划他们的寒假中国之旅。
global_config.setting_summary_en: A cozy university dorm room. Zhang Tianming and Lisa are planning their winter vacation trip to China.

character_definitions.name: 张天明
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A friendly and thoughtful Chinese male university student in his early 20s, with short black hair, wearing a casual sweater.

character_definitions.name: 丽莎
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A curious and enthusiastic American female university student in her early 20s, with light brown hair, wearing a comfortable hoodie.

hanzi: 寒假快到了,张天明这几天一直在考虑旅行的事儿。他的父母希望他去他们的家乡南京看看,张天明也很想当丽莎的导游,带她到中国的一些地方走走。除了南京以外,还应该去什么地方呢?为了决定旅行的路线,张天明拿出一张中国地图,看了起来。
pinyin: hánjià kuài dào le zhāng tiānmíng zhè jǐ tiān yìzhí zài kǎolǜ lǚxíng de shìr tā de fùmǔ xīwàng tā qù tāmen de jiāxiāng nánjīng kànkan zhāng tiānmíng yě hěn xiǎng dāng lìshā de dǎoyóu dài tā dào zhōngguó de yìxiē dìfang zǒuzou chúle nánjīng yǐwài hái yīnggāi qù shénme dìfang ne wèile juédìng lǚxíng de lùxiàn zhāng tiānmíng náchū yì zhāng zhōngguó dìtú kàn le qǐlái
translation: Winter break is almost here. For the past few days, Zhang Tianming has been thinking about taking a trip. His parents want him to visit their hometown, Nanjing. Zhang Tianming would also like to be Lisa's tour guide and take her to a few places in China. Besides Nanjing, where else should they go? To decide on the itinerary, Zhang Tianming took out a map of China and started to look at it.

scenes.scene_goal: 介绍主角张天明和他的旅行计划困扰，引出关键道具——中国地图。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 冬日午后，大学宿舍里。张天明坐在书桌前，若有所思地看着窗外。他脑海中闪过父母在南京的画面，以及和朋友丽莎一起旅行的快乐场景。他下定决心，从书架上拿出一张中国地图，在桌上摊开。
scenes.setting_en: A cozy university dorm room in the afternoon. A calendar on the wall shows winter is approaching.
scenes.camera_movement: Start with a medium shot of Tianming looking thoughtful. Quick cuts to his imagination (parents in Nanjing, traveling with Lisa). Follow him as he gets the map and spreads it out.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A young Chinese man, Zhang Tianming, sits at his desk in a cozy dorm room, looking thoughtfully out the window. A brief, dream-like montage: an image of his parents in a traditional Nanjing setting, then an image of him and a young American woman, Lisa, laughing on a trip. He then stands up, pulls a large map of China from a bookshelf, and spreads it across his desk. Cinematic, warm lighting.

hanzi: 丽莎,你来看看中国地图,看到哪儿去旅行好。
pinyin: Lìshā nǐ lái kànkan Zhōngguó dìtú kàn dào nǎr qù lǚxíng hǎo
translation: Lisa, come take a look at the map of China. Let's see where would be a good place to travel.

scenes.scene_goal: 张天明邀请丽莎加入他的旅行计划。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 张天明站在摊开的中国地图前，回头朝房间另一边的丽莎招手，脸上带着期待的微笑，邀请她一起看地图。
scenes.setting_en: Same university dorm room. A large map of China is spread across a desk.
scenes.camera_movement: Camera is focused on Tianming and the map. He turns and gestures off-screen. Rack focus to Lisa in the background, who looks up from a book.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Zhang Tianming stands over a large map of China on his desk. He turns with a smile and gestures for his friend Lisa, who is sitting in the background reading a book, to come over. The focus shifts from him to her as she looks up, intrigued.

voice_over.speaker: 张天明
voice_over.hanzi: 丽莎,你来看看中国地图,看到哪儿去旅行好。
voice_over.pinyin: Lìshā nǐ lái kànkan Zhōngguó dìtú kàn dào nǎr qù lǚxíng hǎo
voice_over.translation: Lisa, come take a look at the map of China. Let's see where would be a good place to travel.

hanzi: 看地图?你这个主意不错,我也正想学学中国地理呢。我对中国地理了解得太少了。
pinyin: kàn dìtú nǐ zhè ge zhǔyi búcuò wǒ yě zhèng xiǎng xuéxue Zhōngguó dìlǐ ne wǒ duì Zhōngguó dìlǐ liǎojiě de tài shǎo le
translation: Look at a map? That's a good idea. I've been wanting to learn a little about Chinese geography, too. I know too little about Chinese geography.

scenes.scene_goal: 丽莎热情地回应邀请，并表达了自己对学习中国地理的兴趣。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎放下书，快步走到书桌旁。她好奇地看着地图，然后开心地对天明说，这个主意很棒，她也正想了解中国地理。
scenes.setting_en: Same university dorm room, by the desk with the map.
scenes.camera_movement: Track Lisa as she walks to the desk. Close-up on her face as she looks at the map with excitement, then pull back to a two-shot as she speaks to Tianming.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Lisa puts down her book and walks eagerly to the desk. She leans over the map, her expression curious and excited. She looks at Tianming and speaks enthusiastically, gesturing towards the map, saying she's happy to learn about Chinese geography.

voice_over.hanzi: 看地图?你这个主意不错,我也正想学学中国地理呢。我对中国地理了解得太少了。

voice_over.translation: Look at a map? That's a good idea. I've been wanting to learn a little about Chinese geography, too. I know too little about Chinese geography.

scenes.scene_description_cn: 张天明微笑着点点头，然后用手指在地图的东南部找到了南京，指给丽莎看，并解释说这是他父母的家乡，然后询问丽莎的意见。

voice_over.pinyin: Zhōngguó zhème dà wǒmen de hánjià nàme duǎn dàodǐ qù nǎr lǚyóu hǎo ne?
voice_over.translation: China is so big, and our winter break is so short. Where would be the best place for us to visit?

hanzi: 我觉得去云南最好不过了。
pinyin: wǒ juéde qù Yúnnán zuìhǎo búguò le
translation: I think there's no better place to go than Yunnan.

scenes.scene_goal: 张天明自信地提出去云南的建议。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 镜头切换到张天明，他微笑着，自信地用手指指向地图的西南部一个省份。
scenes.setting_en: Cozy university dorm room. A large, detailed map of China is spread across a wooden table.
scenes.camera_movement: Pan from Lisha to Tianming, then follow his hand as it points to the map.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Cut to a medium shot of a young man (Zhang Tianming) smiling. He confidently points to the southwestern corner of a map of China. The camera follows his finger to the spot labeled 'Yunnan'.

voice_over.speaker: 张天明
voice_over.hanzi: 我觉得去云南最好不过了。
voice_over.pinyin: wǒ juéde qù Yúnnán zuìhǎo búguò le.
voice_over.translation: I think there's no better place to go than Yunnan.

hanzi: 云南在哪里?……我找到了,在这儿,是一个省,在西南部。那儿冬天不冷吗?
pinyin: Yúnnán zài nǎli wǒ zhǎodào le zài zhèr shì yí ge shěng zài xīnánbù nàr dōngtiān bù lěng ma
translation: Where's Yunnan? ... I've found it. Here it is. It's a province in the southwest. Isn't it cold there in the winter?

scenes.scene_goal: 丽莎在地图上找到云南，并对当地冬天的气候提出疑问。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎凑近地图，用手指寻找。她很快找到了，然后抬起头，带着好奇和一丝疑虑的表情看着张天明。
scenes.setting_en: Cozy university dorm room, focused on the map on the table.
scenes.camera_movement: Start with a top-down shot of the map as Lisha's finger searches, then tilt up to a close-up of her face.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Close-up on the map as Lisha's finger traces and finds 'Yunnan'. The camera tilts up to her face. She looks up from the map, her expression changing from concentration to curiosity and a little doubt.

voice_over.speaker: 丽莎
voice_over.hanzi: 云南在哪里?……我找到了,在这儿,是一个省,在西南部。那儿冬天不冷吗?
voice_over.pinyin: Yúnnán zài nǎli? ... wǒ zhǎodào le, zài zhèr, shì yí ge shěng, zài xīnánbù. nàr dōngtiān bù lěng ma?
voice_over.translation: Where's Yunnan? ... I've found it. Here it is. It's a province in the southwest. Isn't it cold there in the winter?

hanzi: 听说云南有些地方四季如春,一年四季都不冷不热。
pinyin: tīngshuō Yúnnán yǒuxiē dìfang sìjìrúchūn yìniánsìjì dōu bù lěng bù rè
translation: I hear that some places in Yunnan are like spring all year round, neither too cold nor too hot.

scenes.scene_goal: 张天明描述云南宜人的气候，打消丽莎的顾虑。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 张天明微笑着解释。随着他的话语，画面上叠化出云南春天般的美景：绿色的梯田和盛开的鲜花。
scenes.video_prompt_en: Medium shot of Zhang Tianming speaking reassuringly. As he talks, a beautiful, dreamlike visual of a lush green valley with blooming flowers under a blue sky (Yunnan landscape) fades in and overlays half of the screen, then fades out.

voice_over.speaker: 张天明
voice_over.hanzi: 听说云南有些地方四季如春,一年四季都不冷不热。
voice_over.pinyin: tīngshuō Yúnnán yǒuxiē dìfang sìjìrúchūn, yìniánsìjì dōu bù lěng bù rè.
voice_over.translation: I hear that some places in Yunnan are like spring all year round, neither too cold nor too hot.

hanzi: 我记得电视里说过,云南风景好,少数民族多,是个旅游的好地方。
pinyin: wǒ jìde diànshì lǐ shuō guo Yúnnán fēngjǐng hǎo shǎoshù mínzú duō shì ge lǚyóu de hǎo dìfang
translation: I remember hearing on TV that Yunnan has beautiful scenery, many ethnic minorities, and is a great place for tourism.

scenes.scene_goal: 丽莎回想起电视上关于云南的介绍，对这个提议更加心动。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎的表情由恍然大悟转为兴奋。她旁边出现一个风格化的电视屏幕小图标，快速闪过云南的风景和身着民族服饰的人们。
scenes.video_prompt_en: Close-up on Lisha's face as she remembers something. Her eyes widen and she smiles. A small, stylized graphic of a vintage TV screen appears briefly next to her head, flashing quick cuts of beautiful mountain scenery and people in colorful ethnic minority clothing.

voice_over.speaker: 丽莎
voice_over.hanzi: 我记得电视里说过,云南风景好,少数民族多,是个旅游的好地方。
voice_over.pinyin: wǒ jìde diànshì lǐ shuō guo, Yúnnán fēngjǐng hǎo, shǎoshù mínzú duō, shì ge lǚyóu de hǎo dìfang.
voice_over.translation: I remember hearing on TV that Yunnan has beautiful scenery, many ethnic minorities, and is a great place for tourism.

hanzi: 好,云南,决定了,就去云南。
pinyin: hǎo Yúnnán juédìng le jiù qù Yúnnán
translation: Alright, Yunnan. It's decided. We'll go to Yunnan.

scenes.scene_goal: 张天明拍板决定，两人达成共识，愉快地确定了旅行目的地。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 张天明看到丽莎兴奋的样子，开心地笑了。他果断地在地图上的“云南”位置上敲了一下，做出最终决定。丽莎也开心地点头。
scenes.camera_movement: Pull back from a medium shot to a wider shot to include both characters' happy reactions.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Medium shot of Zhang Tianming smiling broadly. He makes a decisive gesture, firmly tapping 'Yunnan' on the map. The camera pulls back to a wider shot showing Lisha nodding enthusiastically. They both look happy and excited about their decision.

voice_over.speaker: 张天明
voice_over.hanzi: 好,云南,决定了,就去云南。
voice_over.pinyin: hǎo, Yúnnán, juédìng le, jiù qù Yúnnán.
voice_over.translation: Alright, Yunnan. It's decided. We'll go to Yunnan.

global_config.teaching_style: A friendly and structured guide, breaking down dialogue and vocabulary for foundational understanding. The tone is encouraging and clear, focusing on comprehension before diving into complex grammar.
global_config.visual_style: Clean, card-based animations with text overlays and subtle motion graphics.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the context of the conversation: planning a winter break trip.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's begin with the first sentence. It sets the scene for our lesson. The text says [zh:寒假快到了,张天明这几天一直在考虑旅行的事儿]. [zh:寒假] means 'winter break'. [zh:快到了] means 'is almost here'. Zhang Tianming has been continuously, [zh:一直], considering, [zh:考虑], matters related to travel, [zh:旅行的事儿]. This opening line establishes the main theme: making travel plans.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 寒假快到了,张天明这几天一直在考虑旅行的事儿。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hánjià kuài dào le, Zhāng Tiānmíng zhè jǐ tiān yìzhí zài kǎolǜ lǚxíng de shìr.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Winter break is almost here. For the past few days, Zhang Tianming has been thinking about taking a trip.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The holiday period during the winter season for schools.
To think about something carefully in order to make a decision.
The act of traveling. It's a general term for going on a journey.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a calendar flipping to winter, with a character thinking about travel destinations.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the first travel suggestion from Zhang Tianming's parents.

narration.subtitle_en: The story continues, explaining two motivations for the trip. First, [zh:他的父母希望他去他们的家乡南京看看]. His parents, [zh:父母], hope, [zh:希望], he will go visit their hometown, [zh:家乡], which is Nanjing. The word [zh:家乡] is a key term, referring to one's place of family origin. The verb [zh:看看] means 'to take a look', suggesting a casual visit.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他的父母希望他去他们的家乡南京看看...
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tā de fùmǔ xīwàng tā qù tāmen de jiāxiāng Nánjīng kànkan...
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: His parents want him to visit their hometown, Nanjing...

highlight_words.explanation_en: A formal term for 'father and mother'.
To want something to happen or be true.
The town or city where one's family originates from. It's composed of [zh:家] (home) and [zh:乡] (countryside/hometown).

segments.visual_notes: Show a thought bubble from the parents with an image of Nanjing.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand Zhang Tianming's personal motivation for the trip.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, we learn about Zhang Tianming's own idea. The sentence says [zh:张天明也很想当丽莎的导游,带她到中国的一些地方走走]. He also really wants, [zh:也很想], to be Lisa's tour guide, [zh:当...导游]. He wants to take her, [zh:带她], to walk around, [zh:走走], some places in China. The verb [zh:走走] is a reduplicated form, implying a relaxed and casual walk or tour.

on_screen_text.focus_text: ...张天明也很想当丽莎的导游,带她到中国的一些地方走走。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: ...Zhāng Tiānmíng yě hěn xiǎng dāng Lìshā de dǎoyóu, dài tā dào Zhōngguó de yìxiē dìfang zǒuzou.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: ...Zhang Tianming would also like to be Lisa's tour guide and take her to a few places in China.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A person who guides tourists. [zh:导] means to guide, and [zh:游] means to travel or tour.
To lead or carry someone or something to a place.

highlight_words.translation: to take a walk, to walk around
highlight_words.explanation_en: A duplicated verb suggesting a casual, leisurely walk or stroll.

segments.visual_notes: Show Zhang Tianming with a guide flag next to Lisa, with famous Chinese landmarks in the background.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the central question driving the trip planning.

narration.subtitle_en: Now, Zhang Tianming faces a dilemma. He asks himself, [zh:除了南京以外,还应该去什么地方呢]. The structure [zh:除了...以外] means 'besides' or 'in addition to'. So, 'Besides Nanjing, where else, [zh:还], should we go, [zh:应该去什么地方呢]?' The particle [zh:呢] at the end softens the question, making it sound more contemplative.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 除了南京以外,还应该去什么地方呢?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Chúle Nánjīng yǐwài, hái yīnggāi qù shénme dìfang ne?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Besides Nanjing, where else should they go?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A structure used to exclude or include something from a group.
Indicates obligation or advisability.

segments.visual_notes: Show a map with a pin on Nanjing, and question marks popping up over other parts of China.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the action taken to solve the planning problem and the use of 'qilai'.

narration.subtitle_en: To find an answer, Zhang Tianming takes action. [zh:为了决定旅行的路线,张天明拿出一张中国地图,看了起来]. The word [zh:为了] means 'in order to', showing purpose. So, in order to decide the travel itinerary, or [zh:路线], he took out a map of China and 'started to look at it', [zh:看了起来]. The phrase [zh:起来] here indicates the beginning of the action of looking. It's a very useful grammar point we'll explore more later.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 为了决定旅行的路线,张天明拿出一张中国地图,看了起来。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wèile juédìng lǚxíng de lùxiàn, Zhāng Tiānmíng ná chū yì zhāng Zhōngguó dìtú, kàn le qǐlái.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: To decide on the itinerary, Zhang Tianming took out a map of China and started to look at it.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates the purpose of an action.
A planned path or course of travel. [zh:路] means road, and [zh:线] means line.
[zh:地] means earth or ground, and [zh:图] means picture or drawing.

grammar_points.explanation_en: Indicates the beginning of an action.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a character pulling out a large map of China and starting to study it.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to invite someone to join an activity.

narration.subtitle_en: Zhang Tianming now invites Lisa to join him. He says, [zh:丽莎,你来看看中国地图,看到哪儿去旅行好]. He calls her name and says '[zh:你来看看]', which means 'you come and take a look'. Then he suggests the purpose: '[zh:看到哪儿去旅行好]', which literally means 'see where to go travel is good'. It's a way of saying 'Let's see where would be a good place to travel.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 丽莎,你来看看中国地图,看到哪儿去旅行好。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lìshā, nǐ lái kànkan Zhōngguó dìtú, kàn dào nǎr qù lǚxíng hǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Lisa, come take a look at the map of China. Let's see where would be a good place to travel.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An invitation to come and look at something. The reduplication of 'kan' softens the tone.

segments.visual_notes: Show Zhang Tianming gesturing towards the map, inviting Lisa to look.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express agreement and interest in a topic.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa responds positively. She says, [zh:看地图?你这个主意不错,我也正想学学中国地理呢]. First, she confirms the activity, '[zh:看地图]?' or 'Look at a map?'. Then she praises the idea: '[zh:你这个主意不错]' means 'That's a good idea'. Finally, she expresses her own interest: '[zh:我也正想学学中国地理呢]', 'I've also been wanting to study Chinese geography'. The word [zh:正] emphasizes that this is something she was just thinking about.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 看地图?你这个主意不错,我也正想学学中国地理呢。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Kàn dìtú? Nǐ zhège zhǔyi búcuò, wǒ yě zhèng xiǎng xuéxue Zhōngguó dìlǐ ne.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Look at a map? That's a good idea. I've been wanting to learn a little about Chinese geography, too.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A thought or suggestion as to a possible course of action.
A common way to say something is good. Literally 'not wrong'.
The study of the physical features of the earth. [zh:地] is 'earth' and [zh:理] is 'principle' or 'study'.

segments.visual_notes: Lisa's face lights up with a lightbulb icon above her head.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express one's level of knowledge about a subject.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa then explains why she's interested. She says, [zh:我对中国地理了解得太少了]. This sentence uses the structure '[zh:对]...[zh:了解]' to mean 'to know about' something. She feels her knowledge of Chinese geography is 'too little', [zh:太少了]. The particle [zh:得] connects the verb [zh:了解] with the description of how well she knows it. This is a common way to express humility about one's knowledge.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我对中国地理了解得太少了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ duì Zhōngguó dìlǐ liǎojiě de tài shǎo le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I know too little about Chinese geography.

highlight_words.translation: to know about, to be informed
highlight_words.explanation_en: To have knowledge or information about something.
An expression indicating the quantity is insufficient.

segments.visual_notes: Show a silhouette of Lisa's head with a nearly empty bar labeled 'Geography Knowledge'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to propose a sequence of actions and describe a location.

narration.subtitle_en: Zhang Tianming begins the planning. He says, [zh:我们先到南京,我父母的家乡……在这里,在中国的东南。然后你想去哪儿?]. He uses the '[zh:先]...[zh:然后]' structure, meaning 'first... then...'. First, they'll go to Nanjing. He points it out on the map, saying '[zh:在这里]', 'it's here', and specifies its location: '[zh:在中国的东南]', 'in southeastern China'. Finally, he asks Lisa for her input: '[zh:然后你想去哪儿?]', 'Then where do you want to go?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我们先到南京...在这里,在中国的东南。然后你想去哪儿?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒmen xiān dào Nánjīng... zài zhèli, zài Zhōngguó de dōngnán. Ránhòu nǐ xiǎng qù nǎr?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Let's first go to Nanjing... It's here, in southeastern China. Then where do you want to go?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common structure to describe a sequence of events.
A cardinal direction, combining [zh:东] (east) and [zh:南] (south).

segments.visual_notes: Animate a line on the map starting at their current location and moving to a pin on Nanjing in the southeast.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to identify a place and make a suggestion.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa spots a city on the map and makes her first suggestion. She asks, [zh:这是哈尔滨,对吗?]. 'This is Harbin, right?'. The phrase [zh:对吗] is a simple way to ask for confirmation. After identifying it, she makes a direct suggestion using the particle [zh:吧], which softens the command into a proposal: [zh:去哈尔滨吧], 'Let's go to Harbin.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这是哈尔滨,对吗?去哈尔滨吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhè shì Hā'ěrbīn, duì ma? Qù Hā'ěrbīn ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: This is Harbin, right? Let's go to Harbin.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A major city in Northeast China, famous for its winter ice festival.
A tag question used to ask for confirmation.
A sentence-final particle that indicates a suggestion, proposal, or softened command.

segments.visual_notes: Lisa's finger points to Harbin on the map in the far north.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express surprise at a sudden or drastic suggestion.

narration.subtitle_en: Zhang Tianming is taken aback by Lisa's suggestion. He exclaims, [zh:你一下子要从东南边跑到最北边去?]. The key word here is [zh:一下子], which means 'all of a sudden' or 'in an instant'. He is surprised by the huge geographical jump she proposes, from the [zh:东南边], 'southeastern part', all the way to the [zh:最北边], 'northernmost part'. The verb [zh:跑] (to run) is used here figuratively to emphasize the long distance.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你一下子要从东南边跑到最北边去?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ yíxiàzi yào cóng dōngnánbian pǎo dào zuì běibian qù?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You want to go from the southeast to the northernmost part all of a sudden?

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb that emphasizes the speed or abruptness of an action or change.
A structure indicating a range in space or time.
A superlative prefix, used to indicate the highest degree.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a dotted line jumping dramatically from Nanjing in the southeast to Harbin in the northeast on the map.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to explain the reason for a suggestion and use 'jí le' for emphasis.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa explains her reasoning. She says, [zh:昨天电视里介绍哈尔滨的冰灯了,好看极了]. She saw something on TV yesterday, [zh:昨天电视里]. The TV show introduced, [zh:介绍], Harbin's famous [zh:冰灯], or 'ice lanterns'. She describes them as '[zh:好看极了]', which means 'gorgeous' or 'extremely beautiful'. The phrase [zh:极了] is placed after an adjective to express the highest degree, similar to 'extremely' in English.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 昨天电视里介绍哈尔滨的冰灯了,好看极了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zuótiān diànshì lǐ jièshào Hā'ěrbīn de bīngdēng le, hǎokàn jí le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Harbin's ice lanterns were featured on TV yesterday. They're gorgeous.

highlight_words.explanation_en: To present something or someone to others.
Sculptures or lanterns made of ice, a specialty of Harbin. [zh:冰] is 'ice' and [zh:灯] is 'lantern'.
Used after an adjective for emphasis, meaning 'to the utmost degree'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a TV screen displaying beautiful, glowing ice sculptures.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to agree with a point while introducing a counterargument.

narration.subtitle_en: Zhang Tianming acknowledges her point but raises a practical issue. He uses the pattern '[zh:好看是好看]', which means 'They are beautiful, it's true, but...'. This is a great way to concede a point before disagreeing. The problem? '[zh:哈尔滨现在冷得不得了]', 'Harbin is incredibly cold right now'. The phrase [zh:不得了] after an adjective means 'extremely' or 'terribly'. His suggestion is '[zh:夏天再去吧]', 'Let's go in the summer instead.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 冰灯好看是好看,可是哈尔滨现在冷得不得了,夏天再去吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Bīngdēng hǎokàn shì hǎokàn, kěshì Hā'ěrbīn xiànzài lěng de bùdéliǎo, xiàtiān zài qù ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The ice lanterns are beautiful, it's true, but Harbin is incredibly cold right now. Let's go in the summer.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A concessive structure (Adj. + 是 + Adj.) used to acknowledge a fact before presenting a contrasting point.
Used after a verb or adjective to indicate an extreme degree.
The warmest season of the year.

segments.visual_notes: Show the beautiful ice lanterns, then transition to a shivering character and a thermometer dropping to a very low temperature.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to propose an alternative plan using a different mode of transport.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisa is flexible and comes up with a new idea. She says, '[zh:好吧]', 'Alright'. Then she proposes, '[zh:不去北边,那坐火车去西边吧]', 'If we're not going north, then let's take a train to the west'. She introduces a new mode of transport, [zh:坐火车] (take a train). She explains her new destination choice: '[zh:听说新疆是个很特别的地方]', 'I hear Xinjiang is a very special place'. [zh:听说] is a useful phrase meaning 'I heard that...'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好吧。不去北边,那坐火车去西边吧,听说新疆是个很特别的地方。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hǎo ba. Bú qù běibian, nà zuò huǒchē qù xībian ba, tīngshuō Xīnjiāng shì ge hěn tèbié de dìfang.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Alright. If we're not going north, then let's take a train to the west. I hear Xinjiang is a very special place.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'fire vehicle', a term originating from steam locomotives.

highlight_words.translation: to be told; I hear that...
highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to report information that one has heard from others.
A large, autonomous region in the northwest of China.

segments.visual_notes: On the map, the pin on Harbin disappears. A new pin appears on Xinjiang in the west, with a train icon moving towards it.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to politely reject a suggestion by giving multiple reasons.

narration.subtitle_en: Zhang Tianming again points out some problems. He explains that Xinjiang is in the [zh:西北] (northwest) and '[zh:十月就开始冷起来了]', 'it starts getting cold in October'. Notice [zh:冷起来了], again showing the start of a state. So, '[zh:现在去不合适]', 'it's not suitable to go now'. He adds another reason using [zh:另外] (besides): '[zh:坐火车太花时间]', 'taking the train takes too much time'. He concludes with '[zh:下次再去吧]', 'Let's go next time!', a polite way to postpone a plan.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 新疆在西北,十月就开始冷起来了,现在去不合适。另外,坐火车太花时间,下次再去吧!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xīnjiāng zài xīběi, shíyuè jiù kāishǐ lěng qǐlái le, xiànzài qù bù héshì. Lìngwài, zuò huǒchē tài huā shíjiān, xiàcì zài qù ba!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xinjiang is in the northwest. It starts getting cold in October, so it's not a good time to go now. Besides, taking the train takes too much time. Let's go next time!

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates that something is inappropriate for a particular situation.
Used to introduce an additional point or reason.
Literally 'to spend time'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar flipping to October with a snowflake icon over Xinjiang. Then show a clock with its hands spinning rapidly next to the train icon.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to suggest an activity and name China's two most famous rivers.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence proposes a boat trip on China's two most famous rivers. It starts by identifying them: [zh:这是长江] means 'This is the Yangtze River,' and [zh:这是黄河] means 'This is the Yellow River.' The second part, [zh:我们坐船去看看], is a suggestion: 'Let's take a boat to go see.' The word [zh:或者] means 'or,' connecting the two options. Finally, [zh:风景] means 'scenery,' and [zh:吧] is a particle used to make a friendly suggestion.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这是长江,这是黄河。我们坐船去看看长江或者黄河的风景吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhè shì Chángjiāng, zhè shì Huánghé. Wǒmen zuò chuán qù kànkan Chángjiāng huòzhě Huánghé de fēngjǐng ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: This is the Yangtze River, and this is the Yellow River. Let's take a boat to see the scenery of the Yangtze River or the Yellow River.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'Long River,' it is the longest river in Asia and the third-longest in the world.
The second-longest river in China, often called the 'cradle of Chinese civilization.'
A noun for any kind of watercraft. 'To take a boat' is [zh:坐船].
Refers to a scenic landscape. It's composed of [zh:风] (wind) and [zh:景] (view/scenery).

segments.visual_notes: Show an animated map of China with the Yangtze and Yellow Rivers highlighted. An icon of a boat can appear and travel along them.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express that something takes a long time.

narration.subtitle_en: Here's a response to the suggestion. The sentence begins with [zh:坐船], 'taking a boat.' The key phrase is [zh:要花很长的时间]. [zh:花] means 'to spend,' so [zh:花时间] means 'to spend time.' [zh:很长] means 'very long.' So, the whole phrase means 'it would take a very long time.' This is a common way to talk about the duration of an activity.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 坐船要花很长的时间。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zuò chuán yào huā hěn cháng de shíjiān.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: A boat trip would take a very long time.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb [zh:花] means 'to spend' when used with time or money.
An adjective describing length, either in space or time.

segments.visual_notes: An animation of a clock with its hands spinning rapidly next to a boat icon.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the general direction of China's rivers and how to express direction of movement.

narration.subtitle_en: This part explains a key geographical fact. It starts with [zh:中国的河流], 'China's rivers.' The adverb [zh:大多] means 'mostly.' The structure [zh:从西往东流] describes the direction: 'flow from west to east.' The sentence then presents a hypothetical situation using [zh:如果], 'if.' It contrasts the flow by saying if you travel [zh:从东往西走], 'from east to west,' the time, [zh:时间], will be [zh:更长], 'even longer.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 中国的河流,大多从西往东流,如果从东往西走,时间更长。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhōngguó de héliú, dàduō cóng xī wǎng dōng liú, rúguǒ cóng dōng wǎng xī zǒu, shíjiān gèng cháng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Most of China's rivers flow from west to east. If we travel from east to west, it'll take even longer.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A collective noun for rivers in general or all rivers in an area. A single river is often just [zh:河].
An adverb meaning 'for the most part.' It's placed before the verb.
A verb describing the movement of liquids, like a river.
A pattern used to indicate the direction of movement or travel.

segments.visual_notes: Show a simplified map of China with arrows indicating rivers flowing from the high west to the low east.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask for a reason using 'wèishénme'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a classic 'why' question. It starts with [zh:为什么], which means 'why.' The rest of the sentence repeats the statement from the previous line: 'China's rivers mostly flow from west to east.' The particle [zh:呢] is added at the end of the question to soften the tone and indicate curiosity. So, the full question is asking for the reason behind this geographical fact.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 为什么中国的河流大多从西往东流呢?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wèishénme Zhōngguó de héliú dàduō cóng xī wǎng dōng liú ne?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Why do most of China's rivers flow from west to east?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The standard way to ask 'why' in Chinese. It literally means 'for what?'

segments.visual_notes: A large question mark animation appears over the map of China's rivers.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn key geographical terms and understand the reason for the rivers' flow.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence answers the 'why' question by explaining China's topography. It starts with [zh:因为], 'because.' It explains that [zh:中国西部], 'the western part of China,' is [zh:高原], 'plateaus.' It adds that there you'll find the world's tallest mountain, [zh:世界上最高的山]. The word [zh:而] introduces a contrast: 'by contrast,' the east, [zh:东边], consists of [zh:平原], 'plains,' and [zh:大海], 'the ocean.' This difference in elevation is why the rivers flow from west to east.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 因为中国西部是高原,那儿有世界上最高的山。而东边是平原和大海。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Yīnwèi Zhōngguó xībù shì gāoyuán, nàr yǒu shìjiè shàng zuìgāo de shān. Ér dōngbiān shì píngyuán hé dàhǎi.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The western part of China is plateaus; there, you'll find the world's tallest mountain. By contrast, in the east are plains and the ocean.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'high flat-land.' Describes a high-elevation, flat area.
Literally 'flat flat-land.' Describes a low-lying, flat area.
Literally 'big sea.' Refers to the ocean or a large sea.
A conjunction used to connect two clauses that show a contrast.

segments.visual_notes: An animated cross-section of China showing high mountains and plateaus in the west sloping down to plains and the sea in the east.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express an observation about location.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence makes an observation about China's cities. It starts with [zh:好像], which means 'it seems that.' The observation is that [zh:大城市], 'big cities,' are all [zh:靠海], 'close to the coast,' or [zh:离海不远], 'not far from the sea.' The structure [zh:离...不远] is a useful way to say something is near a landmark. This sets up the examples that follow.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好像大城市都靠海或者离海不远。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hǎoxiàng dà chéngshì dōu kào hǎi huòzhě lí hǎi bù yuǎn.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: It seems that the big cities are all close to the coast or not far from the sea.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to express an impression or a guess, similar to 'it looks like' or 'it seems.'
Literally 'to lean on the sea.' It means to be located on or very near the coast.
A common structure to express proximity. [zh:离] means 'distance from.'

segments.visual_notes: Show a map of China's coast, with dots appearing for major cities.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the names of major Chinese cities and how to list examples by region.

narration.subtitle_en: This part provides examples to support the previous observation. It starts with [zh:你看], 'Look.' Then it lists cities by region. [zh:在北边], 'in the north,' we have Beijing and Tianjin. [zh:东南边], 'in the southeast,' there are Shanghai and Nanjing. And [zh:南边这里], 'here in the south,' are Guangzhou and Shenzhen. This is a common way to point out examples on a map or in a list.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你看,在北边,这是北京、天津;东南边,这儿有上海、南京;南边这里,是广州和深圳。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ kàn, zài běibiān, zhè shì Běijīng, Tiānjīn; dōngnánbian, zhèr yǒu Shànghǎi, Nánjīng; nánbian zhèlǐ, shì Guǎngzhōu hé Shēnzhèn.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Look, in the north, there are Beijing and Tianjin; in the southeast, here are Shanghai and Nanjing; and here in the south are Guangzhou and Shenzhen.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Directional word. [zh:北] is north, [zh:边] means side.
A compound direction, combining [zh:东] (east) and [zh:南] (south).
Directional word. [zh:南] is south.

segments.visual_notes: On the map, highlight each city as its name is mentioned, grouped by region (North, Southeast, South).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to agree with someone and describe population distribution.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence starts with an agreement: [zh:你说的没错], which means 'You're right' or 'What you said is correct.' It then explains that China's [zh:人口], 'population,' is [zh:主要], 'primarily' or 'mainly,' located in the [zh:东部], 'eastern part,' and [zh:南部], 'southern part.' Notice the use of [zh:部] here, which refers to a larger region, as explained in the language notes.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你说的没错。中国的人口主要在东部和南部。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ shuō de méi cuò. Zhōngguó de rénkǒu zhǔyào zài dōngbù hé nánbù.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You put it correctly. China's population is primarily in the east and south.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'not wrong.' A common way to agree with someone.
Composed of [zh:人] (person) and [zh:口] (mouth), it means population.
An adverb used to indicate the main or most important aspect.
[zh:部] refers to a region or part of a country.

segments.visual_notes: Show the map of China with the eastern and southern coastal regions shaded to represent high population density.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use 'ne' to contrast different topics and describe regional characteristics.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence explains why the population is sparse in other areas. It uses a special structure with the particle [zh:呢]. First, it introduces the southwest: [zh:西南呢], 'As for the southwest...' and explains it has many mountains and plateaus. Then, it introduces the northwest: [zh:西北呢], 'As for the northwest...' and says it has many deserts. The particle [zh:呢] creates a pause and shifts the topic, creating a contrast. Because the [zh:自然条件], 'natural conditions,' are not good, the conclusion is [zh:所以人口比较少], 'so the population is relatively sparse.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 西南呢,高山、高原多,西北呢,沙漠多,自然条件不太好,所以人口比较少。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xīnán ne, gāoshān, gāoyuán duō, xīběi ne, shāmò duō, zìrán tiáojiàn bú tài hǎo, suǒyǐ rénkǒu bǐjiào shǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: As for the southwest, there are numerous big mountains and plateaus. The northwest, on the other hand, has many deserts with unfavorable natural conditions. That's why the population there is relatively sparse.
on_screen_text.notes: The particle 'ne' is used here to introduce a new topic for comparison.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Using a topic followed by [zh:呢] is a way to introduce a subject for comment or contrast.
A noun for a desert region.
A phrase referring to the natural environment of a place.
A conjunction used to indicate a result or conclusion.

segments.visual_notes: On the map, highlight the Southwest with mountain icons, then the Northwest with desert icons. Show fewer population symbols in these areas.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to say something is 'a bit like' something else.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a simple sentence for making a comparison. It starts with [zh:我觉得], 'I think.' The core comparison is made with the structure 'A [zh:和] B [zh:有点儿像]'. Here, A is [zh:中国的地形], 'China's topography,' and B is [zh:美国], 'America.' The phrase [zh:有点儿像] means 'is a bit like' or 'somewhat resembles.' It's a great way to draw a soft comparison between two things.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我觉得中国的地形和美国有点儿像。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ juéde Zhōngguó de dìxíng hé Měiguó yǒudiǎnr xiàng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I think China's topography is a bit like America's.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Composed of [zh:地] (earth, ground) and [zh:形] (shape, form).
Used here to link two nouns in a comparison.
[zh:像] means 'to resemble,' and [zh:有点儿] means 'a little bit.'

segments.visual_notes: Show a split screen with simplified topographical maps of China and the USA, highlighting similarities (e.g., mountains in the west, plains in the east).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to agree, add information, and compare quantities.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence agrees and adds more information. [zh:你说的有道理] means 'You have a point' or 'What you said makes sense.' [zh:还有] means 'also' or 'in addition.' The speaker then compares the [zh:面积], 'area,' which is [zh:差不多], 'similar,' but the [zh:人口], 'population,' is [zh:美国的四倍多], 'more than four times that of the U.S.' The word [zh:可是] means 'but,' introducing the contrast between area and population.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你说的有道理。还有,中国和美国的面积也差不多,可是人口是美国的四倍多。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ shuō de yǒu dàolǐ. Háiyǒu, Zhōngguó hé Měiguó de miànjī yě chàbuduō, kěshì rénkǒu shì Měiguó de sì bèi duō.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You have a point. Also, China and the U.S. are similar in area, but China's population is more than four times that of the U.S.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'to have reason/logic.' Used to agree that someone's point is logical.
Refers to the surface area of a place.
Literally 'the difference is not much.' A very common phrase for approximation.
Used for multiplication, as in [zh:四倍], 'four times.'

segments.visual_notes: Show two country outlines (China, USA) of similar size. Then, show population icons: one for the USA, and four-plus for China.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to talk about hearsay and describe crowded places.

narration.subtitle_en: This part describes a consequence of China's large population. It starts with [zh:听说], 'I've heard,' indicating the information is secondhand. It describes what happens during holidays, [zh:过节或者放假的时候]. At that time, [zh:旅游景点], 'tourist spots,' are [zh:到处都是人山人海], 'everywhere are seas of people.' [zh:人山人海] is a very vivid idiom. The final phrase, [zh:挤得很], means 'extremely crowded,' with [zh:挤] meaning 'crowded' or 'to squeeze.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 听说,过节或者放假的时候,中国的旅游景点到处都是人山人海,挤得很。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tīngshuō, guò jié huòzhě fàng jià de shíhou, Zhōngguó de lǚyóu jǐngdiǎn dàochù dōu shì rén shān rén hǎi, jǐ de hěn.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I've heard that during holidays or vacations, tourist spots in China are packed with seas of people and extremely crowded.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'hear-say.' Used to report something you've heard from others.
A four-character idiom meaning a huge crowd. It literally translates to 'people mountain, people sea.'
An adjective describing a place packed with people or things.

segments.visual_notes: An animation of a famous landmark (like the Great Wall) being filled with a massive crowd of stick figures.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the meaning and composition of the idiom 'rén shān rén hǎi'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's take a closer look at the idiom [zh:人山人海]. This is a 'chengyu,' or four-character idiom, which are common in Chinese. It literally means 'people mountain, people sea.' Can you picture it? A crowd so large it looks like a mountain of people and a sea of people. It's a very descriptive way to say a place is extremely crowded. You'll often hear it used to describe train stations, shopping centers, or tourist spots during holidays.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 人山人海
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: rén shān rén hǎi
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: a sea of people; a huge crowd

highlight_words.explanation_en: The character for person.
The character for mountain.
The character for sea.

segments.visual_notes: Visually deconstruct the idiom. Show the character '人' multiplying to form a '山' shape, and then a '海' shape.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a simple phrase to express surprise or ask for confirmation.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a very short and common response. [zh:是吗] literally means 'Is it?' but it's used to express surprise or doubt, similar to saying 'Really?' or 'Is that so?' in English. It's a simple way to react to new or surprising information and keep the conversation going.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 是吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Shì ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Really? / Is that so?

segments.visual_notes: A simple text animation with a character showing a surprised expression.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express relief and talk about timing.

narration.subtitle_en: This final sentence expresses relief. It starts with [zh:还好], which means 'fortunately' or 'luckily.' The speaker explains why they are lucky: [zh:我们到中国的时候], 'when we get to China,' the students [zh:还没放假], 'won't be on vacation yet.' The word [zh:还没] means 'not yet.' It also adds that it [zh:也不是过年过节], 'won't be the New Year or other holidays.' This implies they will avoid the huge crowds just discussed.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 还好,我们到中国的时候,学生还没放假,也不是过年过节。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Háihǎo, wǒmen dào Zhōngguó de shíhou, xuéshēng hái méi fàngjià, yě búshì guònián guòjié.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Fortunately, when we get to China, the students won't be on vacation yet, and it won't be the New Year or other holidays.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Expresses relief that a situation is not as bad as it could have been.
Indicates that an action or state has not occurred up to the present moment.

highlight_words.translation: celebrate New Year and other holidays
highlight_words.explanation_en: A general term for the holiday season, combining 'celebrate the New Year' and 'celebrate festivals.'

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar with holiday dates NOT circled, and a smiley face icon.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express a dilemma about making a choice, especially for travel.

narration.subtitle_en: The dialogue opens with a classic travel dilemma. The speaker says [zh:中国这么大], 'China is so big,' using [zh:这么] to mean 'so' or 'this much'. This is contrasted with [zh:我们的寒假那么短], 'our winter break is so short.' The core of the question is [zh:到底去哪儿旅游好呢]. The word [zh:到底] means 'after all' or 'in the end,' and it's used here to press for a definitive answer to the question, 'where is a good place to travel to?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 中国这么大,我们的寒假那么短,到底去哪儿旅游好呢?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhōngguó zhème dà, wǒmen de hánjià nàme duǎn, dàodǐ qù nǎr lǚyóu hǎo ne?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: China is so big, and our winter break is so short. Where would be the best place for us to visit?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A compound word: 寒 (hán) means 'cold' and 假 (jià) means 'vacation' or 'holiday'. So, 'cold vacation' is winter break. Summer break is 暑假 (shǔjià).
An adverb used in questions to press for a final or underlying answer, similar to asking 'what on earth' or 'where exactly'.
Specifically implies traveling for pleasure or sightseeing. The character 游 (yóu) means 'to wander' or 'to tour'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a large map of China shrinking, then a calendar showing a very short winter break.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the structure '最...不过了' for making a strong recommendation.

narration.subtitle_en: Here we get a very strong suggestion. The line starts with [zh:我觉得], 'I think'. The key part is the phrase [zh:最好不过了]. This structure means 'nothing could be better than...' or 'it's the absolute best'. So, [zh:去云南最好不过了] means 'going to Yunnan is the best possible choice'. It's a powerful way to express a recommendation, much stronger than just saying something is 'very good'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我觉得去云南最好不过了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ juéde qù Yúnnán zuì hǎo búguò le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I think there's no better place to go than Yunnan.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A province in Southwest China. Its name means 'South of the Clouds'.
A set phrase using '最' (most) and '不过了' (cannot be surpassed) to mean 'the absolute best'. We will cover this grammar point in more detail later.

segments.visual_notes: Show a map of China with Yunnan province highlighted and a 'Best Choice!' stamp appearing on it.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask for, find, and state a location on a map.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence shows a character's thought process. First, a question: [zh:云南在哪里], 'Where is Yunnan?'. Then, the speaker finds it: [zh:我找到了], 'I found it,' followed by [zh:在这儿], 'It's here.' The speaker then identifies it as [zh:一个省], 'a province.' Finally, they specify its location: [zh:在西南部], 'in the southwest.' Notice the components of the direction: [zh:西] for west, [zh:南] for south, and [zh:部] for 'part' or 'region'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 云南在哪里?……我找到了,在这儿,是一个省,在西南部。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Yúnnán zài nǎli? ... Wǒ zhǎodào le, zài zhèr, shì yí ge shěng, zài xīnánbù.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Where's Yunnan? ... I've found it. Here it is. It's a province in the southwest.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The primary administrative division in China, similar to a state in the U.S.
A compound direction word: 西 (xī) means 'west', 南 (nán) means 'south', and 部 (bù) means 'part' or 'region'.

segments.visual_notes: An animation of a magnifying glass searching a map of China and then stopping over Yunnan province.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use a negative question to confirm an assumption.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a follow-up question based on the location. The speaker asks, [zh:那儿冬天不冷吗]. This literally means 'There, in winter, is it not cold?'. Using a negative question like [zh:不冷吗] often implies surprise or seeks confirmation of an assumption. The speaker likely assumes that most places in China are cold in winter and is curious if Yunnan is an exception.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 那儿冬天不冷吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nàr dōngtiān bù lěng ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Isn't it cold there in the winter?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A compound word: 冬 (dōng) means 'winter' and 天 (tiān) means 'day' or 'sky'. Together, they mean the winter season.
An adjective used to describe temperature. Its opposite is 热 (rè), meaning 'hot'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a thought bubble above the character with a snowy, cold scene inside.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn descriptive phrases for weather, including the idiom '四季如春'.

narration.subtitle_en: This line answers the question about Yunnan's climate. It starts with [zh:听说], meaning 'I heard that...'. The description includes a beautiful four-character idiom: [zh:四季如春]. This means 'four seasons like spring,' a classic way to describe a place with a perpetually pleasant climate. The sentence reinforces this by adding [zh:一年四季都不冷不热], which means 'all four seasons of the year, it's neither cold nor hot'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 听说云南有些地方四季如春,一年四季都不冷不热。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tīngshuō Yúnnán yǒuxiē dìfang sìjì rú chūn, yìnián sìjì dōu bù lěng bú rè.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I hear that some places in Yunnan are like spring all year round, neither too cold nor too hot.

highlight_words.translation: to be told; to hear of
highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'hear and say'. It's used to introduce information that one has heard from others.
A four-character idiom (chengyu). 四季 (sìjì) is 'four seasons', 如 (rú) is 'like', and 春 (chūn) is 'spring'.
A common phrase to describe a perfectly mild and comfortable temperature.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a scene that cycles through icons for all four seasons, but the background remains a pleasant, flowery spring landscape.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn vocabulary for describing tourist attractions like scenery and culture.

narration.subtitle_en: Another character adds more reasons to visit Yunnan. The sentence begins with [zh:我记得], 'I remember,' and cites TV as the source: [zh:电视里说过], 'it was said on TV.' The attractions are listed: first, [zh:风景好], 'the scenery is good.' [zh:风景] is a key word for scenery. Second, [zh:少数民族多], 'there are many ethnic minorities.' This is the official term for China's diverse ethnic groups. The line concludes that it [zh:是个旅游的好地方], 'is a good place for tourism'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我记得电视里说过,云南风景好,少数民族多,是个旅游的好地方。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ jìde diànshì lǐ shuōguo, Yúnnán fēngjǐng hǎo, shǎoshù mínzú duō, shì ge lǚyóu de hǎo dìfang.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I remember hearing on TV that Yunnan has beautiful scenery, many ethnic minorities, and is a great place for tourism.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to the natural views of a place. It's composed of 风 (fēng) 'wind' and 景 (jǐng) 'view'.
The official term for the 55 officially recognized ethnic minority groups in China. It literally means 'minority nationality'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a split screen with beautiful Yunnan landscapes on one side and photos of people in diverse, colorful ethnic clothing on the other.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express a firm and final decision.

narration.subtitle_en: This final line of the dialogue shows a firm decision has been made. It's short and emphatic. It starts with [zh:好], 'Alright'. Then, the speaker confirms the destination: [zh:云南]. The key phrase is [zh:决定了], which means 'It's decided.' The particle [zh:了] here marks the finality of the action. Finally, [zh:就去云南] means 'Then let's go to Yunnan,' with [zh:就] adding a sense of immediacy and resolve.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好,云南,决定了,就去云南。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hǎo, Yúnnán, juédìng le, jiù qù Yúnnán.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Alright, Yunnan. It's decided. We'll go to Yunnan.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb meaning 'to decide' or a noun meaning 'decision'. Here, with '了', it indicates the decision has been made.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a stamp with the characters '决定了' landing decisively on Yunnan on the map.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Starting an Action with 起来
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use 起来 to indicate the beginning of an action or a change of state.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at the complement [zh:起来]. It has two main uses. First, it can follow a verb to show that an action is starting. In the dialogue, Zhang Tianming took out a map and [zh:看了起来], meaning he 'started to look at it'. If the verb has an object, the object goes between [zh:起] and [zh:来]. For example, 'he started to read a magazine' is [zh:他看起杂志来]. Second, [zh:起来] can follow an adjective to indicate the beginning of a new state, often an increase. For instance, the dialogue mentions that Xinjiang [zh:冷起来了], 'started to get cold'. This is common for weather, like [zh:天热起来了], 'the weather is starting to get hot'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Verb + 起来 / Adjective + 起来
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Verb + qǐlái / Adjective + qǐlái
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: To start doing [verb] / To start to become [adjective]

grammar_points.pattern: 张天明拿出一张中国地图, 看了起来。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng ná chū yì zhāng Zhōngguó dìtú, kàn le qǐlái.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Used after a verb to indicate the start of an action.

grammar_points.pattern: 新疆...十月就开始冷起来了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Xīnjiāng... shíyuè jiù kāishǐ lěng qǐlái le.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Used after an adjective to indicate the start of a state.
When there's an object, it goes between 起 and 来.

segments.visual_notes: Animate an arrow pointing up next to adjectives like '热' and '冷' when explaining the 'Adjective + 起来' pattern to signify an increase or onset.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use 而 to connect contrasting clauses.

narration.subtitle_en: The word [zh:而] is a conjunction used to connect two clauses that show a contrast or a different situation. It's a bit more formal or literary than 'but' ([zh:但是]). In the text, Tianming explains China's geography: the west is a plateau, [zh:而东边是平原和大海], 'by contrast, the east has plains and the ocean.' It highlights the difference between the two parts. Another example: [zh:我想去图书馆打工，而我的同屋想去校外打工], 'I want to work at the library, while my roommate wants to work off-campus.' It sets up a clear comparison.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 中国西部是高原, 而东边是平原和大海。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhōngguó xībù shì gāoyuán, ér dōngbian shì píngyuán hé dàhǎi.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The western part of China is plateaus; by contrast, in the east are plains and the ocean.

grammar_points.explanation_en: Connects two clauses with different or contrasting characteristics. It has a literary flavor.

segments.visual_notes: Visually split the screen to show two contrasting images (e.g., mountains vs. coast) with the word '而' in the middle.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the strong superlative structure '最...不过了'.

narration.subtitle_en: To express that something is the absolute best or most suitable, you can use the structure [zh:最] plus an adjective, followed by [zh:不过了]. It's a very strong way of saying 'nothing could be more...'. In the dialogue, Tianming says going to Yunnan is the best idea: [zh:去云南最好不过了], 'There's no better place to go than Yunnan.' It's stronger than just saying [zh:最好]. For another example, if you want to say a gift is perfect, you could say [zh:这个礼物最合适不过了], 'This gift couldn't be more appropriate.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我觉得去云南最好不过了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ juéde qù Yúnnán zuìhǎo búguò le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I think there's no better place to go than Yunnan.

grammar_points.explanation_en: A strong expression meaning 'nothing is more... than...'

segments.visual_notes: Show a trophy or a gold medal icon next to the phrase '最好不过了' to emphasize it's the 'best'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the nuanced difference between the particles 过 and 了.

narration.subtitle_en: We've learned the particle [zh:过] to talk about past experiences. Let's revisit it and compare it with [zh:了]. While [zh:了] simply states that an action occurred, [zh:过] emphasizes the experience and its impact on the present. For example, [zh:我去过中国] means 'I have been to China,' implying I have the experience and knowledge from that trip. In contrast, [zh:我去年去了中国] just states the fact that 'I went to China last year.' In the dialogue, Lisa says [zh:我记得电视里说过], 'I remember it was said on TV.' The use of [zh:过] implies that what was said on TV has informed her current knowledge or opinion about Yunnan.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 你去过中国吗？ A: 我去过。/ 我没去过。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ qùguo Zhōngguó ma? Wǒ qùguo. / Wǒ méi qùguo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: Have you been to China? A: Yes, I have. / No, I haven't.
on_screen_text.notes: 过 (guo) emphasizes experience, while 了 (le) emphasizes completion.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā xuéguo hǎo jǐ nián Zhōngwén.
grammar_points.explanation_en: 过 (guo) indicates a past experience that has an impact on the present.
了 (le) indicates the completion of an action in the past.

segments.visual_notes: Use a timeline visual. For '了', mark a single point in the past. For '过', show a point in the past with an arrow extending to the present, indicating lasting impact or experience.

segments.segment_title: Usage: 为了 (for) vs. 因为 (because)
segments.teaching_goal: To distinguish between 为了, which indicates purpose, and 因为, which indicates cause.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's clarify the difference between [zh:为了] and [zh:因为]. [zh:为了] means 'in order to' or 'for the sake of,' and it introduces the purpose or goal of an action. In the dialogue, [zh:为了决定旅行的路线], 'In order to decide on the itinerary,' Tianming looks at a map. The goal is to decide the route. On the other hand, [zh:因为] means 'because' and introduces the cause or reason for something. Tianming explains why rivers flow east: [zh:因为中国西部是高原], 'because the western part of China is a plateau.' This is the reason. So, [zh:为了] is about the 'why' in terms of purpose, and [zh:因为] is about the 'why' in terms of reason.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 为了 (Purpose) vs. 因为 (Cause)
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wèile vs. yīnwèi
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Distinguishing between purpose and cause.

highlight_words.translation: in order to; for the sake of
highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates the purpose of an action.
Indicates the cause or reason for something.

segments.visual_notes: Use an arrow pointing forward (→) for '为了' to show purpose/goal, and an arrow pointing backward (←) for '因为' to show cause/reason.

segments.segment_title: Usage: 理解 (understand) vs. 了解 (know about)
segments.teaching_goal: To distinguish between 理解, meaning to comprehend, and 了解, meaning to have knowledge of.

narration.subtitle_en: The verbs [zh:理解] and [zh:了解] both relate to understanding, but in different ways. [zh:了解] means 'to know about' or 'to be informed about' something factually. In the dialogue, Lisa says [zh:我对中国地理了解得太少了], 'I know too little about Chinese geography.' She's talking about a lack of factual knowledge. In contrast, [zh:理解] means 'to comprehend' or 'to understand' on a deeper level, like understanding a reason, a concept, or someone's feelings.
